speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2 of the interview of Adam Melcer, M-E-L-C-E-R, taking place on November 29, 1995 in Deerfield Beach, Florida. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where were you from September 1, 1939, and the establishment of the-- of the Warsaw Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, on September we worked-- for 10 weeks, I was in Luck, Poland. It used to be Poland. But the, the Russian army took this part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck, L-U-- L-U-C-K, Luck. And I was living in the hotel, which was owned by my family. For 10 weeks I was there. And the Russian army was there. As a matter of fact, I started working in a Ukrainian newspaper as a compositor for a couple of weeks. And then when I heard that my wife gave birth to the baby-- it was 10 weeks later-- I came back to Warsaw. I smuggled to Warsaw. Of course, it was not easy to go back either.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got back to Warsaw-- can you describe your daily life in Warsaw, when you got back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard, tragic life there. I mean, the population was-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the life under the Germans was very bad. The Germans were running around and picking up people on the street to work. And some didn't even come back. So we didn't know what happened to them, to the people. And the thing is, my printing was opened up because the-- before the war-- a couple of weeks before the war, I bought a machine-- a printing machine from a German company. And the guy who sold me the machine became the Gauleiter of printing of whole Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I owe him money. I have to pay monthly payments for the machine. So I paid him monthly payments to him. And he kept my printing open in Warsaw, which was one of two-- was open during this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as I paid him the money, my printing was open. When I paid up all I owe him, he closed the printing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this gentleman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a German--

speaker: interviewer
text: --German Gauleiter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you to meet up with any famous-- infamous, Nazis while working in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had the-- [SIGHS] once a meeting with Himmler when I was working in the other printing after they closed my printing. He took away the machine, this German. I don't know what they did with it. Maybe he sold it again to another Polish printing. And the rest of the printing was transferred to, to a larger printing-- one printing which was-- the, the SS organized for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In this printing was my part of, of machinery and types. And then I became a worker in this printing from the SS. So-- and I worked there all the time till the uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jews allowed out of the ghetto to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: This printing-- no, this happened after the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, this printing-- no, later they closed the ghetto. And we have--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they close the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: About '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to-- we got an apartment in the part of the-- special part of the ghetto where only the workers for this printing worked. Was on [NON-ENGLISH]. And from there, we have to walk every day. The printing was located outside of the ghetto, between the ghetto and the Polish section. This was a-- nobody lived in this part of the-- of the city where the printing was. And we walked-- every day we walked from the [NON-ENGLISH] to the printing, assisted by German guard, and back to the housing where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there curfews in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there curfews at, at night, you know, hours that you couldn't be outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a curfew. I think was 9 o'clock curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your living space in the ghetto in this housing that you had, what it was like to live there, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we have one apartment. And we lived together with my, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my wife's brother-in-law. They have also a child, which was six weeks older than my daughter-- also a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls? [NON-ENGLISH]. Now she is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And-- OK. And what were the cooking arrangements and the sanitary arrangements where you were living now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we tried to keep the apartment the best way we could. It was not bad. I mean, it was not dirty. We kept it as possible in a good condition. And that's all I can say. It was crowded, of course. This is the special building where all the workers, we worked for the SS in, in the-- around the ghetto, in the factories outside the ghetto-- lived in these buildings where we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to keep warm there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have to keep warm. I mean, we didn't have any coal and such kind of stuff. We couldn't keep warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was food obtained?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have rations. All the people who lived in Warsaw at that time have rations from the Jewish community. Besides, we had our-- our dinner we have delivered in the printing. So this way we, we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a black market?

speaker: interviewee
text: There probably-- it was a black market, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any community life in the ghetto in terms of providing institutions such as schools and hospitals?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some underground schools in the ghetto. Of course there was a hospital. It was a Jewish community, which took care on the hospital and the-- on the orphans-- orphanages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we kept, still, in certain political groups together. And we tried to keep a cultural [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life. There were even theaters in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to a play or a concert in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, you have to have the money. Not everybody has money to go to the theaters. There was a lot of Jewish-- poor Jewish people in the ghetto. And the people tried to make a living, whatever they could do. It was a very hard living in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were Jewish life-cycle events observed, like births and marriages and bar mitzvahs and even deaths?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, there were some of it in the ghetto. Still Jewish people got married and were bar mitzvahs. It was a communal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I worked in the printing, which was by-- was the supervision of the SS and Polizei, the Germans. And then, as I mentioned, Himmler came once and visited to-- visited this printing. He walked around and talked to some-- the organizers of the printing and the supervisor for the printing. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid at all for your labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no pays. We got some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the working conditions like? Can you describe the working conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were treated all right in the printing because we were considered the specialists. And we did work for the-- whatever they ordered to do, as well what they needed. For the SS and for the Jewish community, we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of time, how did you protect your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in this printing, in this building, all of the previous tenants were taken out. And we had an empty apartment up two flights over the printing. And we ordered-- I mean, nobody knew about it except ourselves. My wife and the baby, as well as the other-- another woman, and my sister's-- sister-in-law's baby were hidden there during the day. They stayed all day in this apartment with the children. And my brother-in-law was working in the printing. And I was working in the printing. And the father of the girl was printing-- he was a bookkeeper. He was keeping the books of the printing. And I was in the printing all day long. At night, we carried the babies to the ghetto where we lived. And we stayed there overnight. In the morning, we went to the printing with the babies. And the babies and the women stayed in the-- in this apartment. And we, the men, were working in the printing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you observed to be the fate of children in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very tragic for the children in the ghetto, especially the children who didn't have parents and didn't have any food. And there-- big hunger in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your work permit changed at any time? Was your work changed at any time while you were in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- I would have the good times in the ghetto. According to the others, ours was a special group, which went through in the ghetto without any trouble. Because the Germans need this work. And we were treated a little better than the other outsiders. We haven't-- not any-- we were not in touch with the other groups. Because this group was closed up and special. As, as I said, we go every day to work and every night home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any attempts to organize resistance in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew about it. We heard about it. But we couldn't take any part in it. Because we were the working people, you know, out of the ghetto. And at night they cannot go out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the people involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. We didn't have too much touch with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any prominent people in the ghetto that you knew, or people who later became well-known?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we came in touch with Vladka Meed and her husband, Ben, Ben Meed, Benjamin Meed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet them?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we lived together in the same-- in the ghetto, before they closed out the ghetto-- we lived in the same building where I had my printing. So he was living there, whether he got married to Vladka. So we know this man. And we were in contact with them all the time. As a matter of fact, later on when my girl, like, baby was out with the Polish people and they were on the Polish side, they got in touch with this woman. And they learned-- and they came to our-- when I was later in Radom, after they closed the, the printing and after we were through-- went through a couple of camps-- and as I said, we were called out to go back to Radom because the printing from Warsaw was transferred to Radom. And we-- they came us-- once to visit us. It-- Vladka came us-- to Radom [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to see us. And we talked to her in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she famous for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well she, she was the contact between the population in camp-- in this ghetto, with the outside Resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is the Judenrat established?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- was-- the Judenrat was established by the Germans to do the German work, you know. They have-- they need the help of the Jews to, I mean, keep the Jews in, in place. Like when they ordered the Jews to have to move out the Jews from Warsaw, they said every-- said every day 6,000 Jews have to be transported out of Warsaw to-- supposed to be to work. But really, it was to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Judenrat has to collect the Jews. The Jewish police have to collect the Jews to, to-- and to bring them to this railroad station. That's what they did, the Judenrat. They were working for the Germans. What happened to the first director of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he killed himself. When he learned what the Germans want to do, that we have to pick up 6,000 Jews a day from Warsaw to transfer them outside-- so he killed himself. He said that he doesn't-- he doesn't-- not going to do this. What kind of a work? So he became-- he killed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the job of the Jewish police? How did a person become a Jewish policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish policeman is the same story. Everybody likes-- wanted to have a little job to do something. So they became policemen. I mean, young people-- and they helped out the Germans to, to, to keep the Jews in order, quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally know anyone who became a Jewish policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I knew, policeman. But later on when we were in, in Radom, we knew policemen which worked in Radom Jewish Ghetto. There was also a ghetto in Radom at that time. So we knew some policemen in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supervised the Jewish police, the Germans or the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no not  Germans, not the Poles. The Jewish was all established Jewish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] directors, insofar-- which took care on the Jewish policeman, as they were responsible to the Germans probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have-- the Jewish police-- have any special uniforms or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --insignia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, like a policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the compensation the Jewish police received for their work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any knowledge at this time of what was going on outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean outside the ghetto? In the country?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the rest of Warsaw, in Poland, in the rest of Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody makes a living, so lived normally. The Polish people lived normally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Therefore business was going on. Factories were working. It was a normal life for the Polish people. They were free to go wherever they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware at this time of the existence of the concentration camps? Did you know that, at this point, when you were in the ghetto, that concentration camps existed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware of concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard in the last couple weeks before they're closing the ghettos. We heard about the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard that-- some people even--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943. Even some people came back from concentration camps-- ran off concentration camp, came to Warsaw, and told the story. A lot of people didn't believe it, what's going on. But that time we recognized that we knew that something like this is happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about the extermination programs? Were you beginning to learn about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and that time, we learned about the exterminations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive false identification papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, yes. That was-- I remember when we were in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that is-- oh, OK, we'll talk about that when we get to Radom, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you. What, what actions were in the ghettos? Were there any specific actions? You spoke before about the Yom Kippur action.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was a Yom Kippur action.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we pick up-- they picked up-- the Germans came in and picked up the people on Yom Kippur from the factories, from other places, from the streets, and just took them away, very simple. That's where my-- my sister got lost this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any specific actions that the Germans held that were specifically to capture children that you knew of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not special for children. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any underground newsletters or leaflets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. They were printed-- underground literature-- in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to receive any news from abroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the people rounded up?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rounded up for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --deportation? How was that accomplished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple. They came to, to the building and said, everybody comes down. Who won't come down, we'll go up and shoot them. If we find somebody in the apartment, we shoot them. We got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people went down on the street. And this way, they picked up the people and put them on the street. And they marched down. They marched to, to these Flugplatz-- to the-- down to the-- to the train to taken away. What did the train look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people aware of their destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not everybody was aware. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we knew already what's happening. And a lot of people tried to get off the train, you know, through the window and out of the window. Some succeeded to, to run away. And some got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody volunteer to go on these transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not volunteer because-- I mean, some people were very poor, didn't have any food. They promised to go to work. So they have nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some volunteer. They got afterwards a piece of bread, a bread or two breads for the way. No, they're given a bread before you went into this train, given-- so you have something to eat. But these people volunteered. Describe the first Seder night, April 18, 1943. What happened that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, April 18 was the first night of this uprising. It was the first Seder night, really. And we didn't go to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The printing was closed because we-- see, they closed up this building where we lived. We couldn't go out, even from the building. It was that these people all worked for the Germans, German institutions or Germans factories, or something like the printing, among other things, which were-- the workers lived in the same building.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we heard what's going on. We couldn't leave the building-- Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --till about eight days later. They took us out of the building. The Germans took us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any reading of the Haggadah that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, in any way, suspect what the next day was going to bring?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never knew what the next day will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of the existence of the Hashomer Hatzair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashomer Hatzair, yeah. Sure, I know about the Hashomer Hatzair. And I also knew about, about other organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of that organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashomer Hatzair?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who was the, the head. Maybe Anielewicz, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his role-- Anielewicz's role?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anielewicz was the guy who organized the Resistance. This we heard about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- had you ever met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet up with any of, of the people who were active in the underground or wrote books about what was going to happen, or participated actively in the end of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Everything was very, you know, in secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on April 19, 1943, on Zamenhof Street?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. It was the start of the-- I mean, some Germans came in there. And the Jewish started to fight with them, to kill them, some Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the feeling in the ghetto that first day after they had started the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: The feeling was, there's no other way. I mean, instead to go to camps or the concentration wagons or the death camps. They tried to fight back. But no, no one-- everybody knew what the end will be-- just a fight for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the songs or jokes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no time for jokes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No time for-- no, no time for gallows humor there. Did you ever see any of the leading members of Hitler's regime in Warsaw at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said that the only one I saw was Himmler in the printing when he came and worked on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the day when we were with him at the printing, working.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
